text: Two metals A and B having similar ionic radii react with oxygen to give only monoxides and react with nitrogen to give nitrides. Then A and B respectively are
explanation: Li⁺ (Group 1) and Mg²⁺ (Group 2) have similar ionic radii due to their **diagonal relationship** in the periodic table. Both form only monoxides: Li₂O and MgO. Both also react with nitrogen to form nitrides: \(6\text{Li} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Li}_3\text{N}\) and \(3\text{Mg} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2\). Other Group 1 metals (Na, K) form peroxides or superoxides in addition to monoxides and don't react with N₂ as readily.
name: Questions Item 1

text: \(\text{BF}_3\) is used as a catalyst in several industrial processes due to its
explanation: In \(\text{BF}_3\), boron is \(sp^2\) hybridised and has only **6 electrons** in its valence shell — 2 electrons short of the octet. This makes boron **electron deficient** and capable of accepting a lone pair from a Lewis base. Therefore, \(\text{BF}_3\) is a **strong Lewis acid** and is widely used as a catalyst in Friedel-Crafts reactions, polymerisation, and other organic transformations.
name: Questions Item 2

text: Among the following oxides, which one is weakly basic and yet a powerful oxidant?
explanation: Among the Group 14 dioxides, \(\text{PbO}_2\) is the most basic. More importantly, lead in \(\text{PbO}_2\) is in the +4 oxidation state. Due to the **inert pair effect**, Pb(+4) is less stable than Pb(+2). So \(\text{Pb}^{4+}\) in \(\text{PbO}_2\) has a strong tendency to be reduced to the more stable \(\text{Pb}^{2+}\) (PbO), making \(\text{PbO}_2\) a **powerful oxidising agent** while also being weakly basic.
name: Questions Item 3

text: Name the kind of silicate formed when two silicate units \((\text{SiO}_4)^{4-}\) share one oxygen atom.
explanation: When **two** \([\text{SiO}_4]^{4-}\) tetrahedral units share **one** oxygen atom, the product is the **pyrosilicate** ion \([\text{Si}_2\text{O}_7]^{6-}\). The formula arises as: \(2(\text{SiO}_4) - \text{O} = \text{Si}_2\text{O}_7\). Each silicon retains one bridging oxygen and three non-bridging oxygen atoms. Examples include hemimorphite and thortveitite.
name: Questions Item 4

text: \(\text{SF}_6\) is a kinetically inert substance because
options: Greater difference in electronegativities of S and F
explanation: In \(\text{SF}_6\), sulphur is \(sp^3d^2\) hybridised with **octahedral geometry** and zero dipole moment. The six fluorine atoms completely surround the central sulphur atom, creating steric hindrance. Any incoming reagent cannot approach the sulphur centre because the molecule is **sterically shielded**. This makes \(\text{SF}_6\) kinetically inert despite thermodynamic instability.
name: Questions Item 5

text: A compound X of boron reacts with \(\text{NH}_3\) on heating to give compound Y, called inorganic benzene. X is prepared by treating \(\text{BF}_3\) with lithium aluminium hydride. X and Y are respectively
explanation:
**Step 1:** \(\text{BF}_3 + \text{LiAlH}_4 \rightarrow \text{B}_2\text{H}_6\) (diborane, X) \(+ \text{LiF} + \text{AlF}_3\)

**Step 2:** \(3\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{B}_3\text{N}_3\text{H}_6 + 12\text{H}_2\)

Borazine/borazole (\(\text{B}_3\text{N}_3\text{H}_6\), Y) is called **inorganic benzene** because it is isoelectronic and isostructural with benzene (C₆H₆).
name: Questions Item 6

text: How many compounds can be formed when carbon combines with oxygen?
explanation:
Carbon forms four distinct oxocarbons:

1. **Carbon monoxide (CO)** — neutral oxide
2. **Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — acidic oxide
3. **Carbon suboxide (C₃O₂)** — linear molecule O=C=C=C=O
4. **Mellitic anhydride (C₁₂O₉)** — benzenehexacarboxylic anhydride

So carbon forms **4 different compounds** with oxygen.
name: Questions Item 7

text: Gas X is obtained on heating \(\text{KClO}_3\) with catalyst \(\text{MnO}_2\). This gas (in excess) on reaction with white phosphorus forms an acidic oxide Y. Y on dissolving in water forms compound Z. Identify X, Y and Z.
explanation:
**X:** \(2\text{KClO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{MnO}_2, \Delta} 2\text{KCl} + 3\text{O}_2\) → X = **O₂**

**Y:** \(\text{P}_4 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{P}_2\text{O}_5\) → Y = **P₂O₅** (acidic oxide)

**Z:** \(\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4\) → Z = **orthophosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)**
name: Questions Item 8

text: Number of different bonds present in \(\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}\) is
explanation:
In \(\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}\), the structure has 4 phosphorus atoms each tetrahedrally bonded:

- Each P atom has **1 terminal P=O bond** → 4 P atoms × 1 = **4 P=O bonds**
- 6 bridging oxygens connect P atoms through P−O−P linkages, each contributing **2 P−O single bonds** → 6 × 2 = **12 P−O bonds**

Total: **12 P−O bonds + 4 P=O bonds**.
name: Questions Item 9

text: Which of the following compounds are formed when boron trichloride is treated with water?
explanation:
Boron trichloride (\(\text{BCl}_3\)) readily hydrolyses in water to form **boric acid** and **hydrochloric acid**:

\[\text{BCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3 + 3\text{HCl}\]

This occurs because boron in BCl₃ is Lewis acid and accepts a lone pair from water's oxygen, facilitating bond breaking. This contrasts with CCl₄ which does not hydrolyse due to lack of vacant d-orbitals on carbon.
name: Questions Item 10

text: When \(\text{SbF}_5\) reacts with \(\text{XeF}_4\) to form an adduct, the shapes of the cation and anion in the adduct respectively are
explanation:
\(\text{XeF}_4\) donates F⁻ to \(\text{SbF}_5\):
\[\text{XeF}_4 + \text{SbF}_5 \rightarrow [\text{XeF}_3]^+[\text{SbF}_6]^-\]

- **\([\text{XeF}_3]^+\):** Xe has \(sp^3d\) hybridisation, 3 bond pairs + 2 lone pairs → **T-shaped**
- **\([\text{SbF}_6]^-\):** Sb has \(sp^3d^2\) hybridisation, 6 bond pairs + 0 lone pairs → **Octahedral**
name: Questions Item 11

text: The 'dry ice' is
explanation: **Dry ice** is **solid carbon dioxide (CO₂)**. It is called 'dry' because it sublimes directly from solid to gas at −78.5°C without passing through a liquid phase, leaving no liquid residue. It is used as a refrigerant and in fog machines. Dry ice is a **molecular crystal** held together by van der Waals forces.
name: Questions Item 12

text: Identify the products formed when chlorine reacts with cold and dilute sodium hydroxide solution.
explanation:
Chlorine undergoes **disproportionation** with cold, dilute NaOH:
\[\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\text{cold, dilute}} \text{NaCl} + \text{NaOCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\]

Products: sodium chloride (NaCl) + sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) + water. Cl is simultaneously oxidised to +1 (in NaOCl) and reduced to −1 (in NaCl). With **hot concentrated** NaOH, the product changes to \(\text{NaClO}_3\).
name: Questions Item 13

text: Choose the alkali metal with highest reactivity among the following
explanation: Among alkali metals (Group 1), reactivity **increases down the group** as ionisation energy decreases. The order is Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs. **Caesium (Cs)** has the lowest first ionisation energy among those listed, meaning it most readily loses its valence electron. Note: Fr is even more reactive but is radioactive and not typically considered.
name: Questions Item 14

text: Which oxide among the following cannot act as a reducing agent?
explanation:
An oxide **cannot be a reducing agent** when the central atom is at its **maximum oxidation state** — it cannot be oxidised further.

- SO₂: S is +4, max is +6 → can be oxidised ✓
- NO₂: N is +4, max is +5 → can be oxidised ✓
- **CO₂: C is +4, max is +4 (Group 14)** → **cannot be oxidised** → cannot act as reducing agent ✗
- ClO₂: Cl is +4, max is +7 → can be oxidised ✓

\(\text{CO}_2\) is the answer.
name: Questions Item 15

text: Dilute sodium hydroxide does not react with which of the following?
explanation:
**Boron (B) does not react with dilute NaOH**, though it reacts with concentrated NaOH:
\[2\text{B} + 2\text{NaOH}(\text{conc.}) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Na}[\text{B(OH)}_4] + 3\text{H}_2\]

Al, Ga, and Ti all react with dilute NaOH under various conditions. Boron's unreactivity with dilute NaOH is related to its strong B−O bonds in its protective oxide layer and smaller size.
name: Questions Item 16

text: Which species among the following does NOT show disproportionation reactions?
explanation:
Disproportionation requires a species to be **simultaneously oxidised AND reduced**. This is only possible when the element is NOT at its maximum or minimum oxidation state.

- Cl in \(\text{ClO}_4^-\): oxidation state = **+7** (maximum possible for Cl) → **cannot be oxidised further** → **cannot disproportionate**
- ClO⁻ (+1), ClO₂⁻ (+3), ClO₃⁻ (+5) all have intermediate OS → can disproportionate
name: Questions Item 17

text: Concentrated \(\text{HNO}_3\) turns brown on standing due to formation of
explanation:
Concentrated HNO₃ slowly **decomposes** on standing:
\[4\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2\]

The **nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)** produced is a reddish-brown gas that dissolves back in the acid, giving concentrated HNO₃ its characteristic **yellow-brown colour**.
name: Questions Item 18

text: Which of the following represents the disproportionation of potassium chlorate?
explanation:
**Disproportionation** = simultaneous oxidation AND reduction of the same element.

In option (c): Cl starts at +5 in KClO₃ → in KClO₄ it becomes +7 (**oxidised**) and in KCl it becomes −1 (**reduced**). This is true disproportionation.

Option (a) is thermal decomposition (Cl doesn't change; only O is released). Option (c) occurs at higher temperatures.
name: Questions Item 19

text: A pungent smelling gas A gives dense white fumes with conc. HCl. When A reacts with alkaline solution of a colourless reagent B, a reddish-brown precipitate C is formed. The compounds A, B, C respectively are
explanation:
**A = NH₃:** Pungent; reacts with HCl to give dense white fumes of NH₄Cl: \(\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}\)

**B = K₂HgI₄ (Nessler's reagent):** Colourless solution.

**C = NH₂HgO·HgI (iodide of Millon's base):** NH₃ + Nessler's reagent (alkaline) → **reddish-brown precipitate**. This is the Nessler's test for ammonia.
name: Questions Item 20

text: The possible oxidation states of Group 13 elements is/are
explanation:
Group 13 elements have valence configuration \(ns^2np^1\) and exhibit two oxidation states:

- **+3**: Using all 3 valence electrons
- **+1**: Due to the **inert pair effect** — the \(ns^2\) pair becomes non-bonding, especially for heavier elements (Tl predominantly shows +1)

**+2 is not a stable oxidation state** for Group 13 elements.
name: Questions Item 21

text: Which of the following mineral acids can attack \(\text{SiO}_2\)?
explanation:
**HF** is the only common acid that attacks \(\text{SiO}_2\):
\[\text{SiO}_2 + 4\text{HF} \rightarrow \text{SiF}_4\uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\]

Although HF is a weak acid, the Si−F bond is extremely strong (thermodynamically favourable). This makes HF useful for etching glass and dissolving silicates. HNO₃, H₂SO₄, and HCl do not react with SiO₂.
name: Questions Item 22

text: Which one of the following is NOT present in the nitration mixture?
explanation:
The nitration mixture is 1:1 conc. HNO₃ + conc. H₂SO₄. The active species \(\text{NO}_2^+\) (nitronium ion) is generated:

\[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ + \text{HSO}_4^-\]
\[\text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}\]

Species present: \(\text{NO}_2^+\), \(\text{HSO}_4^-\), \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\). **\(\text{SO}_4^{2-}\) is NOT present** — the strongly acidic conditions keep sulphate protonated as \(\text{HSO}_4^-\).
name: Questions Item 23

text: Find the product of the following reaction when Xe is taken in excess: \(\text{Xe}(g) + \text{F}_2(g) \rightarrow ?\)
explanation:
Product depends on Xe:F₂ ratio and conditions:
- **Xe excess**, 673 K, 1 bar → **XeF₂**
- 1:5 ratio, 673 K, 6 bar → XeF₄
- F₂ excess, 573 K, 60–70 bar → XeF₆

With **excess Xe** at 673 K and 1 bar:
\[\text{Xe(excess)} + \text{F}_2 \xrightarrow{673\text{ K},\,1\text{ bar}} \text{XeF}_2\]

\(\text{XeF}_7\) does not exist.
name: Questions Item 24

text: The formula of white phosphorus in the vapour state is
explanation: White phosphorus exists as **\(\text{P}_4\) molecules** in both solid and vapour states. The \(\text{P}_4\) molecule has a **tetrahedral structure** with 4 phosphorus atoms at the corners connected by 6 P−P bonds. The 60° bond angles cause angular strain making white phosphorus very reactive. Above 800°C, P₄ dissociates into P₂.
name: Questions Item 25

text: A chemical A is used for the preparation of washing soda to recover ammonia. When \(\text{CO}_2\) is bubbled through an aqueous solution of A, the solution turns milky. It is used in white washing due to its disinfectant nature. What is the chemical formula of A?
explanation:
All clues point to **calcium hydroxide Ca(OH)₂**:

1. Recovers ammonia: \(2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\)
2. Milkiness with CO₂: \(\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O}\)
3. Used in white washing with disinfectant properties
name: Questions Item 26

text: The correct order of increasing ionic character among LiH, NaH, KH, RbH, CsH is
explanation:
By **Fajans' rules**, ionic character increases with increasing cation size (decreasing charge density). Going down Group 1, cation size increases: \(\text{Li}^+ < \text{Na}^+ < \text{K}^+ < \text{Rb}^+ < \text{Cs}^+\). Larger cations have lower charge density and reduce covalent character:

\[\text{LiH} < \text{NaH} < \text{KH} < \text{RbH} < \text{CsH}\]
name: Questions Item 27

text: Which halogen can be oxidised by concentrated nitric acid?
explanation:
Reducing power of halogens increases from F to I. **Iodine** has the highest reducing power and is oxidised by conc. HNO₃:

\[\text{I}_2 + 10\text{HNO}_3(\text{conc.}) \rightarrow 2\text{HIO}_3 + 10\text{NO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}\]

Iodine is oxidised to **iodic acid (HIO₃)**. Fluorine cannot be oxidised (most electronegative). Cl and Br are not oxidised by HNO₃ to their oxyacids under normal conditions.
name: Questions Item 28

text:
Choose the correct statement from the following regarding beryllium:

(i) Beryllium is not readily attacked by acids because of the presence of an oxide film on the surface.
(ii) BeO is an amphoteric oxide.
(iii) Beryllium exhibits coordination number more than four.
(iv) Beryllium oxide is purely acidic in nature.
explanation:
**Statement (i) is correct:** Beryllium is protected by a thin, adherent **oxide film** on its surface that prevents further attack by acids (passivation). Be does not oxidise in air even at 600°C.

**Others are incorrect:** BeO is **amphoteric** (not purely acidic). Be has max coordination number of **4** (only 4 available valence orbitals: 2s and 2p). BeO is not purely acidic.
name: Questions Item 29

text: The correct order of acidic character of Group 16 hydrides is
explanation:
Acidic character increases down the group because M−H **bond strength decreases** with increasing atomic size, making H⁺ release easier:

- H₂O: very strong O−H bond → weakest acid
- H₂S → H₂Se → H₂Te: progressively weaker bonds → stronger acids

\[\text{H}_2\text{O} < \text{H}_2\text{S} < \text{H}_2\text{Se} < \text{H}_2\text{Te}\]
name: Questions Item 30

text: The correct order comparing the acid strengths of HF, HCl, HBr and HI is
explanation:
Going down Group 17, H−X bond energy **decreases** (X gets larger), making H⁺ release easier. Acid strength increases from HF to HI:

| Acid | H−X Bond Energy |
|---|---|
| HF | 568 kJ/mol (strongest) |
| HCl | 432 kJ/mol |
| HBr | 366 kJ/mol |
| HI | 298 kJ/mol (weakest) |

Weaker H−X bond → stronger acid: \[\text{HF} << \text{HCl} << \text{HBr} << \text{HI}\]
name: Questions Item 31

text: Which of the following metals is used as a catalyst in Haber's process of ammonia synthesis?
explanation:
In the **Haber process**:
\[\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)\]

Conditions: ~450°C, ~200 atm, **iron (Fe) catalyst** with promoters (K₂O and Al₂O₃). Iron provides good catalytic activity and stability. It lowers the activation energy to achieve acceptable yields in reasonable time.
name: Questions Item 32

text: \(\text{O}_3 + 2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow ?\)
explanation:
Ozone reacts with aqueous KI:
\[2\text{KI}(aq) + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{I}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{KOH}\]

I⁻ is oxidised to **I₂** while O₃ is reduced to O₂. This reaction is used as a **test for ozone** — the I₂ produced turns starch paper blue, confirming ozone's strong oxidising power.
name: Questions Item 33

text: Find the non-semiconductor element among the following.
explanation: Ge, Si, and As are all semiconductors/metalloids with small band gaps. **Lead (Pb)** is a **metal** with overlapping valence and conduction bands — it conducts electricity by metallic conduction, not by semiconductor behaviour. In Pb, the band gap is essentially zero (metallic), so it is a **non-semiconductor**.
name: Questions Item 34

text: Which of the following does not exist?
explanation:
**NeF₂ does not exist.** Neon has the highest first ionisation energy among noble gases — even F₂ cannot oxidise Ne to Ne²⁺. The sum of first and second ionisation enthalpies of Ne far exceeds those of Xe, making neon compounds impossible to form.

All xenon compounds (XeO₃, XeOF₄, XeF₆) are known stable compounds. Neon forms no known stable compounds under normal conditions.
name: Questions Item 35

text: Which of the following statements is NOT correct?
options: The order of boiling points of Group 16 hydrides is \(\text{H}_2\text{S} < \text{H}_2\text{Se} < \text{H}_2\text{Te} < \text{H}_2\text{O}\)
Rhombic sulphur has S₈ molecules while monoclinic sulphur has S₆ molecules
The bond angle in ozone molecule is 117°
explanation:
**Statement (c) is INCORRECT.** Both rhombic sulphur and monoclinic sulphur are **allotropes of S₈** — they both consist of puckered S₈ rings. The difference is only in how these S₈ rings are packed in the crystal lattice, not in molecular formula. Neither contains S₆.

All other statements are correct: reducing power decreases SO₂→TeO₂; H₂O has anomalously high bp due to H-bonding; O₃ bond angle ≈ 117°.
name: Questions Item 36

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

I. \(\text{B}_2\text{H}_6\) is an electron deficient hydride.
II. \(\text{NH}_3\) is an electron rich hydride.
III. NaH is a covalent hydride.
IV. \(\text{YbH}_{2.55}\) is an interstitial hydride.
explanation:
**I — Correct:** B₂H₆ uses **3-centre-2-electron (3c-2e) bridge bonds** — it has fewer electrons than needed for conventional 2-centre bonds → **electron deficient**.

**II — Correct:** NH₃ has a lone pair beyond its bonding requirements → **electron rich**.

**III — Incorrect:** NaH is an **ionic (saline) hydride** (Na⁺H⁻), not covalent.

**IV — Correct:** YbH₂.₅₅ has H in interstitial voids of Yb metal lattice → **interstitial hydride** (non-stoichiometric).

Correct: **I, II, IV**.
name: Questions Item 37

text:
In the following reactions:

\(\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + \text{NH}_3(\text{excess}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} X + \text{H}_2\)

\(\text{NaH} + \text{BF}_3 \xrightarrow{450\text{ K}} \text{NaF} + Y\)

\(\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + Z\)

X, Y and Z are respectively
explanation:
**X:** \(3\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{B}_3\text{N}_3\text{H}_6 + 12\text{H}_2\) → X = **B₃N₃H₆** (borazine)

**Y:** \(2\text{BF}_3 + 6\text{NaH} \xrightarrow{450\text{ K}} \text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NaF}\) → Y = **B₂H₆** (diborane)

**Z:** \(\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_3\text{BO}_3 + 6\text{H}_2\) → Z = **H₃BO₃** (boric acid)
name: Questions Item 38

text:
Assertion (A): \(\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}\) cannot be used to remove moisture from ammonia gas.

Reason (R): \(\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}\) reacts with \(\text{NH}_3\) gas.

The correct answer is
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
explanation:
**Assertion (A) is correct:** P₄O₁₀ cannot dry ammonia because it reacts with it.

**Reason (R) is correct and explains (A):**
\[12\text{NH}_3 + \text{P}_4\text{O}_{10} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4(\text{NH}_4)_3\text{PO}_4\]

Since P₄O₁₀ **chemically reacts** with NH₃ (not just absorbs water), it cannot be used as a drying agent for ammonia. R correctly explains A.
name: Questions Item 39

text: Which one of the following statements regarding helium is NOT correct?
options: It is used to fill gas balloons instead of hydrogen because it is lighter and not flammable
It is used in gas cooled nuclear reactors
It is used to produce and sustain powerful superconducting magnets
It is not used as a cryogenic agent
explanation:
**Statement (d) is INCORRECT.** Liquid helium (bp = 4 K) is widely used as a **cryogenic agent** — it cools superconducting magnets in MRI machines and is essential in low-temperature physics research.

All other statements are correct: He is used in balloons (non-flammable), in nuclear reactor cooling, and in maintaining superconducting magnets.
name: Questions Item 40

text: Which one of the following reactions does NOT occur?
explanation:
**Option (b) does NOT occur as written.** The correct hydrolysis of ClF₃ gives:
\[3\text{ClF}_3 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 9\text{HF} + 3\text{HCl} + 3\text{HOF}\]

The reaction in option (b) incorrectly shows F₂ as a product, which is not formed in this hydrolysis. Reactions (a), (c), and (d) are all correct and well-established.
name: Questions Item 41

text: \(\text{KO}_2\) reacts with water to form A, B, and C. B forms C when it reacts with iodine in basic medium. What are B and C respectively?
explanation:
**Reaction 1:**
\[2\text{KO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \underbrace{2\text{KOH}}_{A} + \underbrace{\text{H}_2\text{O}_2}_{B} + \underbrace{\text{O}_2}_{C}\]

**Reaction 2:** H₂O₂ (B) + I₂ in basic medium:
\[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{I}_2 + 2\text{KOH} \rightarrow 2\text{KI} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \underbrace{\text{O}_2}_{C}\]

H₂O₂ acts as reducing agent (oxidised to O₂). KOH cannot reduce I₂.

**B = H₂O₂, C = O₂**.
name: Questions Item 42

text: Which one of the following methods is used to prepare carbon monoxide on commercial scale?
options: Dehydration of formic acid with conc. \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\)
Direct oxidation of C in limited supply of oxygen
explanation:
**Commercial production of CO** uses the **water gas process** — passing steam over hot coke:
\[\text{C}(\text{coke}) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{steam}) \xrightarrow{\text{Ni}, \Delta} \text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2(g)\]

This produces water gas (CO + H₂) on large scale.

- Option (a): Lab scale only
- Option (b): Not used commercially
- Option (d): CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (gives CO₂, not CO)

Option (c) is the commercial method.
name: Questions Item 43

text:
Which of the following reactions are correct with respect to the formation of products?

I. \(2\text{NaOH} + \text{SO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\)
II. \(2\text{XeF}_4 + 3\text{O}_2\text{F}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{XeO}_3 + 7\text{F}_2\)
III. \(\text{PCl}_5 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 5\text{HCl}\)
IV. \(2\text{NaNO}_2 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{NO} + \text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}\)
explanation:
**I — Incorrect:** Correct reaction: \(2\text{NaOH} + \text{SO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}\) (gives Na₂SO₃, not Na₂SO₄).

**II — Incorrect:** Correct reaction: \(\text{XeF}_4 + \text{O}_2\text{F}_2 \rightarrow \text{XeF}_6 + \text{O}_2\).

**III — Correct:** \(\text{PCl}_5 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 5\text{HCl}\) ✓ (excess water hydrolysis)

**IV — Correct:** HCl decomposes NaNO₂ to give NaCl, NO, NO₂, and H₂O ✓

Correct: **III and IV**.
name: Questions Item 44

text: Chlorine oxidises \(\text{SO}_2\) in the presence of water to give an oxyacid A. Chlorine also oxidises iodine in the presence of water to give an oxyacid B. The oxidation states of S and I in A and B are respectively
explanation:
**Oxyacid A:** \(\text{SO}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}\)

In H₂SO₄: \(2(+1) + x + 4(-2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +6\) → S in A = **+6**

**Oxyacid B:** \(\text{I}_2 + 5\text{Cl}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HIO}_3 + 10\text{HCl}\)

In HIO₃: \((+1) + x + 3(-2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +5\) → I in B = **+5**
name: Questions Item 45

text: White phosphorus is heated with concentrated NaOH in a \(\text{CO}_2\) atmosphere to form a gas A and compound B. When A is bubbled into aqueous \(\text{CuSO}_4\) solution, copper phosphide and C are formed. B and C are respectively
explanation:
**Step 1:** \(\text{P}_4 + 3\text{NaOH} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{CO}_2} \underbrace{\text{PH}_3}_{A} + \underbrace{3\text{NaH}_2\text{PO}_2}_{B}\)

A = **PH₃ (phosphine)**, B = **NaH₂PO₂ (sodium hypophosphite)**

**Step 2:** \(2\text{PH}_3 + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cu}_3\text{P}_2\downarrow + \underbrace{3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}_{C}\)

C = **H₂SO₄**
name: Questions Item 46

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

I. \(\text{Ga}_2\text{O}_3\) is an amphoteric oxide.
II. The dimer of aluminium chloride has three Al−Cl−Al bridge bonds.
III. Boron is a very hard refractory solid of high melting temperature.
explanation:
**I — Correct:** Ga₂O₃ is **amphoteric** — reacts with both acids (→ GaCl₃) and bases (→ NaGaO₂).

**II — Incorrect:** Al₂Cl₆ has **only two** bridging Cl atoms forming **two** Al−Cl−Al bridge bonds, not three.

**III — Correct:** Boron has a very high melting point (~2349 K) and is used as a **refractory material**.

Correct: **I and III**.
name: Questions Item 47

text: Magnesium is burnt in air to form A and B. When B is hydrolysed, C and D are formed. D is the reactant in the manufacture of nitric acid by Ostwald's process. What is C?
explanation:
**In air:**
- \(2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}\) (A)
- \(3\text{Mg} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2\) (B)

**Hydrolysis of B:**
\[\text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\underbrace{\text{Mg(OH)}_2}_{C} + 2\underbrace{\text{NH}_3}_{D}\]

**D = NH₃** is the feedstock for Ostwald's process (4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O).

Therefore **C = Mg(OH)₂**.
name: Questions Item 48

text:
Which of the following reactions can be used to prepare diborane?

I. \(\text{BF}_3 + \text{LiAlH}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{ether}}\)
II. \(\text{BF}_3 + \text{NaH} \xrightarrow{450\text{ K}}\)
III. \(\text{Na}_2\text{B}_4\text{O}_7 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow\)
IV. \(\text{NaBH}_4 + \text{I}_2 \rightarrow\)
explanation:
**I:** \(4\text{BF}_3 + 3\text{LiAlH}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{ether}} 2\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 3\text{LiF} + 3\text{AlF}_3\) ✓ (lab method)

**II:** \(2\text{BF}_3 + 6\text{NaH} \xrightarrow{450\text{ K}} \text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NaF}\) ✓ (industrial method)

**III:** Na₂B₄O₇ + H₂O → gives **boric acid** (H₃BO₃), NOT diborane ✗

**IV:** \(2\text{NaBH}_4 + \text{I}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{diglyme}} \text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 2\text{NaI} + \text{H}_2\uparrow\) ✓ (lab method)

Reactions **I, II, IV** all produce diborane.
name: Questions Item 49

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

I. Germanium exists only in traces.
II. The order of electronegativity of Si, Ge, Sn is \(\text{Sn} > \text{Ge} > \text{Si}\).
III. All Group 14 elements are available in solid state.
explanation:
**I — Correct:** Silicon and carbon are abundant; however, **Ge, Sn, and Pb exist in trace amounts** in the Earth's crust.

**II — Incorrect:** Electronegativity **decreases** down Group 14: C > Si > Ge ≈ Sn > Pb. The correct order is Si > Ge > Sn (not Sn > Ge > Si).

**III — Correct:** All Group 14 elements (C, Si, Ge, Sn, Pb) exist as **solids** at room temperature.

Correct: **I and III**.
name: Questions Item 50
